cross_connections.display_label: Amán Rawson smuggled copies of his Facundo into San Juan in medicine drawers labeled for venereal diseases.
Benjamin Franklin Rawson was his first art student and lifelong friend — they shared a house in Chilean exile.
was his first pupil and lifelong friend

notable.notable_blurb: President of Argentina 1868-1874; author of Facundo

narrative_blocks.header: He wrote the book that Amán Rawson smuggled
narrative_blocks.body: Domingo Faustino Sarmiento wrote Facundo in 1845 while in Chilean exile — a devastating attack on the Rosas dictatorship that was banned inside Argentina. He and Benjamín Franklin Rawson shared a house during that exile. It was Amán Rawson — the Hooker descendant from Massachusetts — who smuggled copies of Facundo back into San Juan hidden in drawers reeking of chemical stench, labeled as remedies for venereal diseases.

narrative_blocks.header: He rose from teacher to president of Argentina
narrative_blocks.body: Sarmiento served as president of Argentina from 1868 to 1874, championing public education on a national scale. He had begun as a provincial schoolteacher in San Juan.

narrative_blocks.header: He taught the Hooker line's Benjamin Rawson
narrative_blocks.body: Sarmiento's very first pupil was Benjamin Franklin Rawson, the San Juan boy of Massachusetts Hooker descent. The two shared a house in Chilean exile and stayed friends for life.

narrative_blocks.header: His Facundo became a Latin American classic
narrative_blocks.body: Written in Chilean exile, Sarmiento's Facundo attacked the dictator Rosas through the life of a gaucho strongman, framing a struggle between civilization and barbarism. It is often called the single most important book of nineteenth-century Spanish America.

narrative_blocks.header: He studied schools across Europe and America
narrative_blocks.body: Chile sent Sarmiento abroad in 1845 to examine education systems, and he returned convinced the United States offered Latin America its model. As president he founded teachers' colleges, libraries, and museums for a largely illiterate country.
